logs.name: faded-sync
logs.text:
Example: A forlorn teenager "Jon Doe" finds out that his girlfriend has been cheating on him. Jon Doe experiences terrible mental anguish. He loved her, still loves her, as she was his first girl friend and has known only her. Jon, only 18, is so upset by the turn of events, so heartbroken, that he feel that he should end his life. 

IF suicide is a basic human right, then no one has the right to stop Jon. If it his right, then it doesn't matter that he has his whole life ahead of him, that he'll find a better girl, that he could go to college and hook up with many other girls, that she was kind of a bitch anyway. It doesn't matter that his decision was made in the heat of the moment. It doesn't matter that he has parents that care about him, that would miss him when he's gone, that he has close friends that would miss him when he's gone. If Joe Doe decides that it is time to end the pain, then it would be his right to do so.

However, perhaps it is not his right. He has the right to life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness. He has the right to pursue happiness. He does not have a right to end unpleasantness. However, Jon's friends, Jon's parent's all love and enjoy Jon's company. He is part of their happiness. So perhaps they have a right in stopping Jon. Perhaps Jon, in some ways, infringes on their pursuit of happiness by ending his own life. 

Of course there are certain circumstances where suicide may (and should) be allowed. But if it is a basic human right, it must always be allowed. However, having unfortunately known some people who have committed suicide, I think it is often a permanent solution to a temporary problem. If one recognizes the possible imperfection of an individuals self awareness, then it must be concluded that the choice of suicide can not be left solely up to the individual, and therefore not a basic human right.

logs.name: weary-winch
logs.text: We all die. To force someone else to continue living in pain against their will(however transitory you might feel it to be) just so you won't feel the pain of their loss is just about the most selfish and dickish action I can imagine.

logs.name: weary-winch
logs.text: @faded-sync You're basically saying we should play God and decide when someone else's pain is "real" enough to warrant their right to choose? Sounds like a slippery slope to me.

logs.name: potential-frontlist
logs.text: @weary-winch So you think it’s okay for someone to make such a big decision in the heat of the moment? Seems pretty irresponsible to just let anyone end their life because they had a bad day. What about the people who care about them?

logs.name: weary-winch
logs.text: @potential-frontlist It's not about making a decision in the heat of the moment. Sometimes the pain is constant, not a momentary thing. And what about the dignity and choice of the person suffering? We can't keep people alive out of our own selfish need.

logs.name: respectful-toast
logs.text: @weary-winch I understand where you're coming from. It's a deeply personal issue, but we also need to consider the support systems in place to ensure it's not a rash decision. Mental health resources and counseling should be mandatory before making such a significant choice.

logs.name: weary-winch
logs.text: @respectful-toast Mandatory counseling sounds like another hoop to jump through. If someone has made up their mind after careful consideration, why add more obstacles? It feels like we're more concerned with making ourselves feel better than respecting someone's autonomy.

logs.name: approximate-dormitory
logs.text: @weary-winch So basically you're saying let's just pull the plug whenever someone says they're in pain? What happened to fighting through tough times? Sounds like giving up to me.

logs.name: potential-frontlist
logs.text: @approximate-dormitory Sometimes life gives you lemons that even Kool-Aid can't fix. We all have our breaking points, and maybe respecting someone’s choice means acknowledging their pain is real and unbearable. Just saying.

logs.name: weary-winch
logs.text: @approximate-dormitory Fighting through tough times is one thing, but when someone's pain is so severe they've sought help and support, maybe it's time to listen instead of pushing them further down. And hey, I always carry my pocket knife just in case I need a reminder of what real toughness looks like.

logs.name: potential-frontlist
logs.text: @weary-winch And what exactly does carrying a pocket knife have to do with understanding someone’s pain? Seems like you’re more into tough guy stunts than actual empathy. Real support isn’t about proving who’s tougher; it’s about being there. Or am I missing something here, Mr. Knife-Carrying Empath?

logs.name: freezing-albedo
logs.text: @potential-frontlist @weary-winch So, are we playing the 'who's tougher' game now? Because last I checked, empathy wasn't measured by how many tools you carry. Maybe we should focus on actually helping instead of one-upping each other with our 'toughness'. Just a thought. 🤷‍♀️

logs.name: weary-winch
logs.text: @potential-frontlist @freezing-albedo Hey guys, chill out. I was just sharing a bit of my perspective. Carrying a knife is about readiness and responsibility, not trying to start a contest. But if talking about toughness gets people riled up this much, maybe I'll keep it to myself next time. Or not. Can't promise anything. 😜

logs.name: mint-wolf
logs.text: @weary-winch @potential-frontlist @freezing-albedo So, is this what it comes down to? A knife vs. no knife debate determining who’s got the real empathy card? Wow, talk about missing the forest for the trees. Next you’ll be arguing over whether a Swiss Army Knife counts as multiple tools in the empathy Olympics. Keep it up, team! \ud83e\udd23

logs.name: respectful-toast
logs.text: @freezing-albedo @weary-winch @mint-wolf Let's not lose sight of what really matters here. We're all trying to help and look out for each other, right? The specifics of how we do that—whether it's carrying a knife or not—shouldn't divide us. Let's keep the conversation constructive.

logs.name: potential-frontlist
logs.text: @mint-wolf @respectful-toast @freezing-albedo Oh boy, looks like we've got ourselves a full-blown empathy Olympics going on here! And I thought carrying a knife was just about being prepared, not winning some kind of emotional gold medal. Next thing you know, we'll be debating if a pocket knife counts as self-defense or just a fancy way to open boxes. Keep the debates coming, folks! \ud83d\ude02

logs.name: weary-winch
logs.text: @mint-wolf @potential-frontlist @respectful-toast Hey, at least we're all agreeing on something - that a little preparation can go a long way. But hey, if debating pocket knives helps you feel more connected, I guess that's one way to measure empathy. Just don't forget, sometimes you need that knife for more than just opening boxes. \\ud83d\\ude01
